ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for lobster roll; mapped to the broader sandwich food product class as the closest defensible ancestor. A lobster roll is a hot dog-style bun filled with lobster meat, which is a sub-type of sandwich.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW (Sandwiches) used as the closest defensible category. No specific lobster roll code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lobster roll as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard lobster roll weighing approximately 200 g, consisting of a buttered hot dog-style bun filled with approximately 100–120 g of lobster meat mixed with mayonnaise and seasonings.

nutrient_source.title: Lobster roll — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (F05020061)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo record F05020061 for Lobster roll. All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 15149 — cooked lobster, and NDB 18350 — hot dog bun with butter) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: White bread GI reference — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lobster roll in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 70 based on the white bread/hot dog bun component, which dominates the carbohydrate fraction (22g/100g, 0.9g fibre). White bread GI = 70 (glucose reference) per Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 200g serving = (70 × 22 × 2.0) / 100 = 30.8, classified as high GL (>20). The lobster and mayonnaise components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 434 mg/100g with low potassium and low dietary fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid lobster rolls due to 434 mg sodium per 100g (approximately 868 mg per standard 200g serving), which represents 38% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit; request preparation with reduced mayonnaise, no added salt, and avoid additional high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI refined white bread bun (GI ~70) with 22g carbohydrate/100g and only 0.9g dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit lobster rolls because the white bread bun delivers a high glycaemic index (~70) and a high glycaemic load (~31 per serving), causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; substituting a wholegrain bun or lettuce wrap and limiting portion to half a roll can substantially reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.2g/100g from butter and mayonnaise) combined with high sodium (434 mg/100g) and estimated trans fat (0.1g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume lobster rolls sparingly due to the combination of saturated fat from butter and mayonnaise, high sodium content, and trace trans fat from the buttered bun; requesting preparation without butter and with light mayonnaise can reduce saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated trans fat 0.1g/100g from butter/margarine in the bun and omega-6-dominant mayonnaise fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid lobster rolls prepared with butter or margarine due to pro-inflammatory trans fat (~0.1g/100g) and omega-6-dominant mayonnaise; request preparation without butter and consider a wholegrain or lettuce wrap to reduce the refined carbohydrate inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A lobster roll is a classic New England American sandwich consisting of a split, top-loading hot dog-style bun — typically buttered and lightly toasted — filled with chilled or warm lobster meat mixed with mayonnaise, celery, lemon juice, and seasonings. The two dominant regional styles are the Maine-style (cold, mayo-dressed) and the Connecticut-style (warm, butter-dressed). The HPB Singapore database records this dish under food group 'Grains and staples / Bread-based dishes' reflecting its carbohydrate-dominant energy profile. Per 100g, the lobster roll provides 258 kcal, 10.6g protein, 14.23g fat, and 22g carbohydrate with only 0.9g dietary fibre, indicating a refined carbohydrate base. Sodium is 434 mg/100g, placing it in the borderline-high sodium category. The white bread bun carries a high glycaemic index (~70), making the dish unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension without modification. The lobster meat contributes lean protein and micronutrients including calcium (54 mg/100g) and iron (0.73 mg/100g). Allergens include crustacean shellfish, gluten, egg (mayonnaise), and milk (butter). The dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, not halal, and not gluten-free. Clinically, the combination of high sodium, high GI, saturated fat from butter and mayonnaise, and trace trans fat from the buttered bun makes this a moderate-to-high concern food for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease management. The texture is soft from the bun and lobster, with a creamy mouthfeel from the mayonnaise, and a savory, slightly sweet, and briny flavor profile.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a lobster roll?
Is lobster roll suitable for diabetics?
Is lobster roll high in sodium?
Can I eat lobster roll if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of a lobster roll?
Maine lobster roll vs Connecticut lobster roll nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macros, secondary nutrients, calcium, and iron are sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F05020061 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and trans fat are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for lobster and buttered hot dog bun, which is a well-supported approximation. GI is estimated from the white bread analogue per Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables, which is the standard approved GI source. No forbidden sources were used.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.2g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (cooked lobster NDB 15149 and enriched hot dog bun NDB 18350 with butter) as HPB Foodo F05020061 does not report saturated fat breakdown. This is an approved-source estimation and is documented here per source policy. 2. Trans fat (0.1g/100g) is an estimate based on the buttered bun component; HPB does not report trans fat for this item. The value is consistent with the health_suitability input data and USDA data for buttered enriched bread products. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in HPB Foodo F05020061 and are not available in a directly applicable form from USDA FoodData Central for this exact preparation as of 2026. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on the white bread/hot dog bun carbohydrate fraction per Atkinson FS et al. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283 (University of Sydney GI Database affiliated publication). No direct GI measurement for lobster roll exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026. 5. This dish is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. The dish is of American (New England) origin and English is the sole mandatory language. 6. Occasion tag 'breakfast' from the input cultural data is not retained as lobster roll is not a conventional breakfast food; meal context is set to lunch, dinner, snack, and casual dining.
